text.title: Feature Flag Cleanup Discipline Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that bind every feature flag to a retirement contract from the moment it is created. Cleanup is a first-class discipline, not a hopeful afterthought.

rule.statement: No flag ships without an expiry date, a named removal owner, and a one-sentence kill criterion stored in a versioned flag-ledger (e.g. `flags.yml` or its remote equivalent). The PR that introduces the flag must add the ledger entry; CI rejects the PR if any of the three fields is empty or missing.

rationale.source: Feature-flag retirement gap in multi-month flows

rule.rationale: Flags created without expiry, owner, or kill criterion become orphaned the moment the original author switches context; the toggle layer becomes a graveyard of permanent forks pretending to be temporary.

rule.statement: Every flag has exactly one of four states: rolling-out, full-on, full-off, kill-switch. Once a flag reaches full-on or full-off and stays there for two sprints, it is automatically marked ready-to-remove and a cleanup ticket is opened against the removal owner.
rule.rationale: A flag that has been fully-on or fully-off for weeks is no longer a flag — it is a dead branch in the codebase. The state machine forces the conversation: keep as kill-switch (rename), or delete.

rule.statement: Every sprint includes a flag-sweep task whose definition of done is: (a) reconcile ledger ↔ flag store ↔ code references; (b) close all ready-to-remove flags; (c) flag any drift (orphan in code, orphan in store, orphan in ledger) as a blocker.
rule.rationale: Without a recurring sweep, the three sources of truth — ledger, flag-service, code — drift apart, and the operator can no longer answer "is this flag still doing anything?" with confidence.

rule.statement: Removal is a single mechanical PR: delete the flag-read in code, delete the ledger entry, archive (not delete) the flag in the flag store with a "removed in PR #N" note. The PR template explicitly forbids bundling flag removal with unrelated feature work.

rationale.source: Hidden tech-debt from accumulated stale flags

rule.rationale: When flag removal is mixed with feature work, the diff hides the deletion and reviewers cannot certify that the toggle is gone; orphan reads survive and the "removed" flag continues to gate behaviour through stale defaults.

rule.statement: Any flag whose expiry date has passed by more than one sprint without removal is escalated: the ledger entry is auto-promoted to status overdue, the next sprint plan is blocked from starting new flag-gated work until at least one overdue flag is killed, and the overdue list is included in the release notes.

rationale.source: Retirement-side discipline closes the rollout-only gap

rule.rationale: An expiry without an escalation is a wish, not a deadline. The "no new flags until one overdue dies" rule keeps the toggle layer's debt budget honest and prevents the long tail of forgotten flags from compounding.

example.title: Birth-to-kill of a migration flag
example.code:
Day 0 (rollout PR): flags.yml entry added: key: orders.v2_pipeline expires: 2026-09-15 owner: ruslan kill_when: "v2 carries >=98% of order events for 14 consecutive days with zero P1 incidents" state: rolling-out
Sprint 6: Metric crossed threshold → state: full-on for 14 days Sprint 8 sweep flips state to ready-to-remove, cleanup ticket opened
Sprint 9: Removal PR #842: - delete flag read in src/orders/pipeline.py - delete flags.yml entry - archive flag in LaunchDarkly with note "removed in PR #842" PR is single-purpose, no other feature work in the diff
Sprint 10 release notes: "Flags retired this release: orders.v2_pipeline. Overdue: 0."
example.note: Every step is mechanical, observable, and produces a paper trail. The flag never enters the long tail of forgotten toggles.
